content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Looking for a movie where the Directors Cut is vastly superior to the Theatrical Release.

**Score:** 44
**Body:** I love movies where the plot is something you think about for awhile after viewing. 

Decided to start watching Directors Cuts to see if it is worth the extra time. 

Some movie types I’ve loved are:

Eternal Sunshine of the Spotless Mind

It’s a Wonderful Life

Harold and Maude 

The Leftovers (an HBO series, not movie)

Being There

Mother!

Grey Gardens 

Fargo

(Only seen the theatrical releases on those)

Decent character development, absurd and tense situations requiring extraordinary resolutions… that kind of stuff. thank you all so much! 

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Kingdom of Heaven

The Abyss

JFK

Aliens
  - Aliens (1986) is like a totally different movie when you compare theatrical to director's cut.

Hadley's Hope feels like people lived there, not just some mess on LV-426.
  - Great call on The Abyss. An All-Timer that has been overshadowed by the rest of Cameron’s Filmography.
2. Waterworld - Ulysses edition

Not a directors cut but an edit that adds about 50mins back to the theatrical version. Was so good the studio picked it up to distribute it
3. Dark City (1998) - This film should ONLY be watched as the director's cut!
4. Kingdom of Heaven
5. Heaven's Gate (1980)

Once Upon a Time in America (1984)
6. LOTR Trilogy, extended cuts.
7. Star Trek: The Motion Picture.
8. Doctor Sleep.
One of the best DC’s I’ll ever watch. 
Flanagan does a Triple Lindy and ties together Kubrick and King’s “The Shining” with so much love and respect while telling this new story.  I was surprised how much I loved the theatrical, but when I watched the DC, I was swooped up and in awe by the detail and care that went into that movie.

## 2. New Year’s Eve movie recommendations for a solo, no-plans night 🍿

**Score:** 18
**Body:** I don’t have any plans this New Year’s and will be spending the night alone. I’m looking for movie recommendations I can watch back-to-back without getting bored or emotionally drained.

Anything works action, thrillers, comfort movies that feel like a warm hug, fun escapism, or engaging stories that pull you in and keep you there. I’m specifically trying to avoid films that are too heavy, introspective, or likely to make me spiral into self-reflection.

Basically, movies that help you forget the clock, the calendar, and the feelings for a few hours. Would love your go-to picks for nights like this.

**Edit:** Thank you so much, everyone! I didn’t expect so many thoughtful recommendations. I’ve added all of them to my watchlist, feeling a lot less alone and very excited for this movie marathon. 🍿🤍

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Hudsucker Proxy is my go-to for New Year’s and fits your criteria. Maybe make it a Coen Bros comedy night with O Brother Where Art Thou, The Big Lebowski, and Raising Arizona.
2. Young Frankenstein (1974)

My Cousin Vinny (1992)

Something about Mary (1998)

Ferris Bueller’s Day Off (1986)
3. Night Of The Comet.
4. My Go-to New Years Movies include:

When Harry Met Sally...

The Apartment 

The Poseidon Adventure (1972)

Trading Places 

Peter's Friends (1992)

Terror Train (1980)
5. Holidate with my babygirl Emma Roberts. It had surprising depth. It's about two people who decide to pair up only for holidays so that they don't get shit from their family about being dateless.
6. My go-to’s for that sitch:

Fletch

Midnight Run

My Cousin Vinny

48 Hours

Beverly Hills Cop

Speed 

The Princess Bride

-

Some other newer ones you might enjoy:

Caught Stealing

Relay
7. I know the title sounds weird but I was thoroughly entertained by the movie “Cowboys and Aliens” on Netflix. Daniel Craig is the star and it’s based on the premise of what would happen if there was an alien invasion in the 1800’s west. It’s surprisingly good and you don’t have to think too much.
8. Stardust

Tucker and Dale vs Evil

Senior Trip

Galaxy Quest

Jay and Silent Bob Strike Back

Dogma
9. The secret life of Walter Mitty
10. **"Nobody"** with Bob Odenkirk. Seriously. You WON'T be disappointed. :)

## 3. UNDERRATED POST APOCALYPTIC MOVIE/SERIES RECOMMENDATIONS

**Score:** 18
**Body:** I’m really into post-apocalyptic movies where only one person or just a few people remain. Ideally, there should be at least one female character, and the story should explore human connection — whether that’s romantic, daughter-like, or purely about survival and loneliness.
I prefer apocalypses that are human-made (science, war, experiments, radiation, etc.), and not zombie-based.

Movies with a slow, emotional, atmospheric tone are my favorite — something similar to The Road, which I really loved. 

Another perfect example for me is Z for Zachariah — it hits everything I’m looking for:
very few survivors
post-apocalyptic sci-fi
emotional tension
survival elements
and even a subtle love triangle

If you know any movies or limited series with a similar vibe, especially grounded sci-fi or realistic post-apocalypse stories, please recommend them.
Thanks in advance 🙌

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. [Station Eleven](https://www.themoviedb.org/tv/90972-station-eleven)
2. The Rover (2014) is pretty good. Guy Pearce and Robert Patterson, more akin to Mad Max 1 though than full on desolate apocolypse
3. Jericho
4. Just watched pluribus it was pretty good
5. I Think We're Alone Now
6. A boy and his dog
7. Fury Road, I am legend, Waterworld, Z for Zachariah,
8. Station Eleven is great. Female lead. One series. 

I have The Road on right now :)
9. The Survivalist 


Its imho the most realistic one out there.
10. It Comes At Night

## 4. Movies where a character has to try and evade suspicion for a crime they committed or are suspected of committing

**Score:** 16
**Body:** Essentially, like the title says I'm looking for movies where a main character has to evade some kind of law enforcement using unique tactics due to them being guilty of a crime or suspected of committing a crime and them not wanting to be found out.

  
Some examples of these would be:

  
No Way Out (1987)

  
Out Of Time (2003)

  
Gattaca (1997)

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The fugitive
2. Minority report
3. Gone girl
4. Shooter (2007)
5. Sneakers
6. L A Confidential
7. Several Hitchcock movies used the "falsely accused man on the run" formula, most notably Saboteur, North by Northwest and Frenzy.
8. The Next Three Days
9. Heat
10. Inside Man (2006)

Enemy of the State (1998)

## 5. Enron: The Smartest Guys in the Room (2005)

**Score:** 12
**Body:** A few weeks ago I made a post on finding movies based on books as it can bring details to the screen, and also gives an opportunity to person to know more.

Looks like the movie

Enron: The Smartest Guys in the Room (2005)

is based on the book "The Smartest Guys in the Room: The Amazing Rise and Scandalous Fall of Enron" by Bethany McLean , Peter Elkind 

I have watched this movie, and it is a great one that shows how greed engulfs the top management in a very short time. It shows how the missteps were behind hidden from employees and shareholders.


PS: I made a project out for finding movies based on books for events that happened this week. It is called RealMovies - https://movies.learntosolveit.com/week/2025-12-02/?exclude=2

### Top 4 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. As a Californian who had my electric bills spike because of these mfers I’m upset the ceo died before he went to prison. Fuck all these people so so much.
  -   Yeah died immediately after the verdict, before the chance to appeal. That let his wife keep a lot of cash. 
   I read to many conspiracies. But,   If I was a guy like that I'd hire a body double early in my career. Pay him enormous money to eventually take the fall. Get him a complete new set of teeth, full xrays and work all under the billionaire's name, then some minor infraction so he gets fingerprinted under the billionaire's name.
   Now he lives like a king, somewhere, until he is  summoned by the king to take the fall.
  "Don't worry" the king tells him. "My great wealth will protect you". Of course, that's not true. The kings wealth is there to protect the king. Once the body double has served his purpose the only thing left is to eliminate any chance of his true identity being discovered. So he dies, suicide, heart attack. Who knows? Dental records and fingerprints confirm the billionaire/king is dead. Real billionaire goes wherever he wants with his new face.
   Told you I read too many conspiracies
    - “Died”
2. If I can suggest a couple antidotes…The Corporation for a dark flick. But The Yes Men for a hilarious look at trolling the corporate world in person before trolling was even a thing.
3. Dumb Money

Jungle

Went to your link. I liked the book The Making of the Atomic Bomb. Didn't know there was a film. Will have to check it out.
  - I think, the movie was not directly based on the book. I will remove that suggestion.
4. Margin Call. 

Too Big To Fail. 

Recount. 

Casino Jack.

---

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. "Charlie Wilson's War" (2007) is one of those movies that i didn't really appreciate how good it was when I first saw it years ago, but as I have gotten older, I absolutely love it now.

**Score:** 710
**Body:** I knew it was a good movie when I rented it back in the day, but thought it was a bit slow. Now that I am older, I can really sit back and appreciate/enjoy the story, the great screenplay by Aaron Sorkin, amazing directing by Mike Nichols (his final film), the phenomenal acting by 2 of the greatest male actors ever (Tom Hanks and Philip Seymour Hoffman), with Julia Roberts and Amy Adams both delivering top-notch performances as well, and the beautiful cinematography. I love all of the different locations that were portrayed in the movie. 

And it being based on George Crile's book *Charlie Wilson's War: The Extraordinary Story of the Largest Covert Operation in History* makes it feel all the more real (I mean, it is a true story, after all). I know there were some artistic liberties taken, but still, it is a fascinating story of how a U.S. Congressman and a random CIA operative lead the efforts (Operation Cyclone) to organize and support/arm the mujahideen during the Soviet-Afghan War (1979-89). As a history lover, it is interesting as hell being able to look back at how that particular conflict had such a massive affect on future world events. All in all, I'd give it a solid 9/10. 

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Hoffman is so good in this
  - No actor had the range of Hoffman. He did Along Came Polly, Capote, Mission Impossible 3, and Charlie Wilson's War in a span of a few years. Unbelievable run
    - His 1997-2000 may be even better

Boogie Nights
Lebowski 
Magnolia
Talented Mr Ripley
State & Main
Almost Famous
2. Gust Avrakotos: Yeah you're fucking Roger's fiance, and you know I know.

Cravely: I'm not... I'm not... I'm not even gonna dignify that with a response.

Gust Avrakotos: Yeah yeah, you're dignifying her in the ass, at the Jefferson Hotel, Room 1210, but let me ask you, the 3000 agents Turner fired, was that because they lacked diplomatic skills as well?
3. As with any script written by Aaron Sorkin, the dialogue is brilliant. One of the few screenwriters working today who can make a movie worth watching solely on the merits of conversations.
4. Worth it for Philip Seymour Hoffman alone. He was AMAZING.
  - Been many years since I've seen it, but I remember his office rant being amazing.
5. Gus Avocado
6. Do you know the story about the Zen Master and the little boy?
7. THAT’S A THICK DOOR!!!

## 2. Brigitte Bardot Dies at 91.

**Score:** 595
**Body:** Brigitte Bardot was a famous French actress, model, and singer who became an international icon in the 1950s and 1960s. She rose to global fame with films like And God Created Woman (1956), which changed the image of women in cinema by portraying freedom, sensuality, and independence. Bardot starred in more than 40 films and also had a successful music career. 

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. She was gorgeous. Fucking mental, but gorgeous.
2. Had talent, the looks, but that’s not enough to redeem how bad of a person she was. We gotta stop glorifying these people, seriously.
  - I don't know anything about her, what did she do?
3. Looks great for 91!
  - And deceased
4. And then she became anti-feminist, racist, and xenophobic. Good riddance.
5. Gorgeous face, ugly heart. What she did to her son…
6. That's a long time being gorgeous
  - She did pay the leathery skin price for sun seeking.
7. This woman was bad right

## 3. Trading Places (1983): Still funny?

**Score:** 245
**Body:** I expected it to feel dated more than 40 years later. It didn’t, at least in my experience. Curious how others feel about it now.

Edit: It might feel like an obvious answer, but it’s still a real question. I believe that comedy isn’t just about whether something once worked, it’s about how the context shapes what we actually find funny.

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. This movie is THE Christmas film here in Italy they always air it on Christmas Eve it’s pretty funny
2. It still holds up, very well cast, and the Philadelphia setting adds some nice local character to the movie.
3. Hold on a second.



FUCK OFF.
4. Sure, like you might find in a bacon, lettuce, and tomato sandwich
  - <*Eddie looks directly into camera*>
5. Referenced it yesterday to my sis about one of the best crossover jokes; Mortimer, we're back!

Oh damn, that was coming to america ofc.
6. Funny and Jaime Lee Curtis!
7. Looking good, Billie Ray!
  - Feeling good, Louis!
8. One of the best of all time in my opinion

## 4. One of the finest biographical dramas ever made.

**Score:** 36
**Body:** Underrated work of Meryl Streep. Definitely career best performance by Cher. Everything about Silkwood is so perfect the story telling, truth, fight against system and it's consequences. Go watch it, if you haven't yet!! 

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Great movie, it does an amazing job of building suspense
2. I highly recommend viewing this film👍
3. Mike Nichols is a great director
4. It's criminal it's not streaming anywhere.
5. Silkwood showers are horrific
6. It's very difficult/impossible to fund online. True and good labor story as well as nuclear adjacent.
  - It's available through Hoopla
7. I forgot this film and now I remember I will definitely watch it again!
8. Good movie. I watched it on Blu Ray a few days ago.
9. That’s like the hottest cast ever

## 5. Anyone else spend more time scrolling for a movie than actually watching it?

**Score:** 30
**Body:** Feels like having endless options somehow makes it harder to relax and just press play.

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. That, and watching trailers.
  - I’ll probably won’t see *Supergirl*, but I’ve seen the trailer 50 times.
2. Streaming services need a random button
  - 100%. I’d use it more than the search bar 😅
3. Usually half a meal time
4. I do the same thing with porn. Either I find one that works eventually or I bore myself to sleep. Win-win.
5. I feel like the user interfaces need to be improved. It’s so hard to browse on many of the apps. I’ve definitely felt that decision paralysis before, too. 

I used to spend a long time at the video store, but I’d eventually end up leaving with a choice. Maybe we need a feature that allows us to carry 5 movies to checkout, but then leave 4 behind.
6. No… because I made a watchlist with 4K + films that keeps growing
7. Part of the problem is that most movies on streaming services are absolute molten garbage. 

I spend time looking for one I think will be bearable. And no I’m not anything close to a film snob, I love good B-movies, sci-fi, crime, anything. 

But so many movies are just boring and badly made.
8. No, I don’t watch much movies anymore. I watch more TV shows but I always have a clear idea what I wanna watch when I scroll so maybe for like 30 seconds I look around and then I find one. But I cannot watch a movie to relax anymore that’s at least me.

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Most stressful Safdie project?

**Score:** 13
**Body:** I just saw Marty Supreme last night and saw many people saying they felt it was "way more stressful" than Uncut Gems. I went in with those expectations.

(Spoilers?)

I thought it was very well done, but in terms of stress, this one had its moments but things were kind of resolved quickly (mostly, I felt) where as in UG I thought things weren't as escapable and
kept escalating until the end. 

It is a different story, but very similar scenes between the two films (the whole dismissive guy with power who's already moved on with begging protagonist comes to mind). Still, Marty Supreme had a bit more heart to me, a nicer ending, and had a lot more breathing room imo

Deeper themes too(?)

The Curse gave me a lot of anxiety, though

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Good Time was hands down the one that stressed me out the most. Uncut Gems and Marty Supreme had some really fun moments along with some stressful ones. But Good Time was uncomfortable for me, especially with his brother with the developmental disability and the underage girl. All incredible movies though.
2. I think Uncut Gems takes the cake for stress. 

MS has some nice “quiet” moments, the montage of the Globetrotters being one of them, it’s funny and gives us some time to relax. Marty giving the tip in the theater is another, just small segments where we let the actors and characters do their thing. The entire ending match isn’t necessarily “stressful” and more of a fun type of drama. 

Uncut Gems is just pure shit-headery on every level. There is no fun to be had during the final basketball game, this isn’t derogatory to the film mind you, but the stakes are all Howie’s making, and the gains are so little in the grand scheme of his life (dude was most likely a millionaire already).
3. With Uncut Gems there’s no relief whatsoever. Whereas with Marty Supreme I felt like there was a little. It’s still really stressful. The world puts obstacles in front of Marty and he then decides to make those obstacles worse himself. And while Marty does “win” and comes home in the end, you just know he’s going to have to face the music and that in itself is stressful.
4. Marty grew by the end. Good movie. One Hustle After Another - even if by every measure you are excellent at what you do, you still have to hustle! Sometimes the hustle becomes the event - like Gwyneth Paltrow’s show, but you still have to be exceptional for anything to happen from it. I dug uncut gems bug the ending was a drag. Good Time had a transcendent ending. I saw Marty S yesterday and it is still percolating. I really dug it. Excellent cast all around. It was too long, though. Too many closed loop or unaddressed side plots, but that’s okay.
5. Uncut Gems, but I find gambling to be incredibly stressful. There is a scene in a film called In America where a dad is losing his savings trying to win an E.T. doll in a carnival game where it is one of the most stressful scenes for me to get through. Hell, I even have trouble with Lost in America.

So yeah, Uncut Gems for me.

## 2. Shadows in the Garden of Eden: Why The Godfather Is Still the Ultimate American Tragedy

**Score:** 9
**Body:** 

Before we see a single frame of violence, before we see the cat in the tuxedoed lap, we hear a line that haunts the entire runtime of the film: "I believe in America." It is spoken not in triumph, but in bitter desperation. When the undertaker Bonasera emerges from the darkness, his plea sets the stage for what is arguably the most profound critique of the American experiment ever committed to celluloid. *The Godfather* is often misremembered as merely a gangster film, a collection of quotable lines and violent set pieces. But at its heart, it is a political tragedy about what happens when the social contract breaks down, and when the bright promise of the rule of law fails to protect those living on the margins.

The brilliance of Francis Ford Coppola’s masterpiece lies in how it frames the Corleone family not as an aberration of society, but as a necessary response to it. In the post-war America depicted in the film, the "legitimate" institutions—the courts, the police, the politicians—are presented as exclusive clubs for the WASP establishment. Justice is a commodity, available only to those with the right pedigree or the deepest pockets. When the courts suspend the sentences of the men who brutalized Bonasera’s daughter, the state has failed its primary duty. Into this vacuum steps Don Vito Corleone. He is not just a crime lord; he is a pre-modern monarch operating within a modern democracy. He offers an alternative judicial system, one based not on cold statutes but on "friendship," loyalty, and the immediate, brutal rectification of grievances. Vito’s world is feudal, warm, and terrifyingly efficient, offering a sense of belonging that the cold machinery of American capitalism denies its immigrant children.

However, the film’s true tragedy lies in the transition from Vito to Michael, a shift that mirrors the modernization of America itself. Vito represented a romanticized, almost Old World form of corruption—he had lines he wouldn't cross, such as the narcotics trade, because he understood that some profits cost too much social capital. Michael, the war hero who initially wanted nothing to do with the "family business," represents the cold, rational efficiency of the modern corporation. His transformation is the film’s dark heart. When Michael kills Sollozzo and the police captain, he isn't just committing murder; he is shedding the illusion that the "straight" world is morally superior to the underworld. He realizes that the Senator and the Judge are just game players like his father, only hypocritical about it.

This realization leads to the film’s most chilling sequence: the baptism. As Michael stands at the altar renouncing Satan, his hitmen are systematically wiping out his enemies across New York. It is the ultimate merger of the sacred and the profane, a visual declaration that in the pursuit of absolute power, the soul is merely collateral damage. By the time the door closes on Kay in the final shot, Michael has secured his family’s safety and dominance, but he has done so by becoming a solitary, icy figure, indistinguishable from the ruthless corporate titans he once despised. He wins the world but loses his humanity, proving that in a system driven by unchecked ambition, the only way to survive the shark tank is to become the biggest shark. *The Godfather* remains timeless because it whispers a comfortable lie and a hard truth: we root for the bad guys because the "good guys" in the halls of justice failed us long ago.

### Top 0 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)


## 3. What Have You Been Watching? (Week of (December 28, 2025)

**Score:** 2
**Body:**  Please don't downvote opinions. Only downvote comments that don't contribute anything. Check out the [WHYBW archives](https://www.reddit.com/r/TrueFilm/wiki/index#wiki_what_have_you_been_watching.3F).

### Top 4 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Ok, i'll bite. Apologies for inevitable typos, this is written rapidly and with little revision:

  
Species (1995), rewatch: Probably my favourite b-movie ever, and no, it's not because we see the gratuitous (though admitedly beautiful) tits of Natasha H. I think this is pretty well crafted for what was a limited budget, some good character interactions, good chemistry between Michael M and the other woman, some weird character moments for Ben K which are never really explained. It's very entertaining and great finale in the sewers. 8/10

Heart Eyes (2025): I very rarely read about movies when i watch them, so had no idea what i was getting into. 5 minutes in, that was not my thing. Stopped it, bye. 1/10

Sentimental Value (2025): Wonderful film, wonderful actresses. I thought it dragged slightly in the middle, but absolutely redeemed by THAT scene. You all know which one i am talking about if you've seen it. "I had you". Absolutely heartbreaking stuff. 8/10

It was just an accident (2025): My best film of the year. 10/10. Beautifully written, relatable characters, funny at times, sad at others. And that ending. I could not breathe for those final couple of minutes.

The Mastermind (2025): I have nothing against slow cinema, but this was not slow cinema, this was nothing cinema. Awful stuff that is not even worth me writing more about. 1/10

Misericorde (2024): What a strange little film. It's actually pretty good once you get into its vibe. The priest is probably my favourite character i've seen on screen this year. Just out there. 9/10

One Battle After Another (2025), rewatch: Caught this one on IMAX on release, liked it well enough, watched again this week. Yeah, it's ok. It's a technical marvel the likes of which few directors can aspire to, and great acting all around, but the story never really engaged me that much. We are introduced to those characters in the prologue, especially the mother (i'm bad with names), but all of them are rapidly written out of the film, and PTA then asks me to care about Willa. I never saw a reason why. Willa is your typical rebelllious teenager that shouts a lot, and i never related. By the final 30 minutes i was checked out, especially when in the finale, we have Willa in a car chase with an assassin we barely met five minutes ago. The stakes were never really that high. The epilogue was awful. On the one hand, we have Willa reading a letter from the mother who abandonned accompanied with saccarine music, one the other, we have a completely unnecessary scene where Lockjaw is not actually dead, but then is gassed by his would-be right-wing supremacists chummies. It is as if i needed a hammer to remind me that right-wing supremacists are nazis. Whatever. 7/10
2. [**WEEK # 260:**](https://tilbageidanmark.tumblr.com/tagged/movies)

"*Shall I make you hot cocoa?...*"

Anders Thomas Jensen is my favorite Danish filmmaker, both as a director and screenwriter. The new dark comedy **THE LAST VIKING** is only his 6th feature film, a certain continuation of the motifs and dynamics from his last masterpiece 'Riders of Justice'. Similar group of broken and traumatized people, played by his usual ensemble of co-workers; curly-long-haired Mads Mikkelsen is mentally-sick half-wit who's convinced he's John Lennon, Nikolaj Lie Kaas is his egoistical brother with a serious anger-management problems, Nicolas Bro (a fantastic character actor!) is a ruthless psychopath, a real "Heavy" [Ha-ha...], and Lars Brygmann continues his unhinged role as Lennart from 'Riders'.

It plays again with dysfunctional families, a bunch of random misfits who end up living together in some kind of a healed community, certifiably crazy (but lovable) people, and a Beatles cover band that plays ABBA songs too.

It's not the best ATJ, but I think I will like it even more on re-watch. [The trailer.](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tFzcPgFXQZA) 7/10.

I also tried to watch Mads Mikkelsen's other new movie, 'Dust Bunnies', but it was terrible, and I could only last about 5 minutes.

🍿

"*Whose crack are we talking about, Gene...?*"

First watch: Robert Altman's tragicomedy **SHORT CUTS**, another of his late career 'Ensemble' pieces: 22 main characters whose strange stories are loosely connected, all stereotypical Angelinos of the 1993 type. Some of the individuals are better told than others: The main characters are all men who are assholes, Tim Robbins, Peter Gallagher, Matthew Modine, Jack Lemmon... and some of these plots are labored. But others are magical, dramatic and cinematographic (the introduction of the jazz house at 1:04!). Personally, these were my first years living in Southern California, so a lot of the background resonated with me. Long Live limo driver Tom Waits.

9/10 for the parts that worked.

🍿

**BLUE MOON** is the second of two new Richard Linklater artist-biopics that he released simultaneously a few months ago. Like his other, 'Nouvelle Vague', it's a fictional "hangout" period piece about 'important' creators. I was not familiar with lyricist Lorenz Hart before, so the interesting details here were about Sardi's restaurant, seeing cameos of young George Roy Hill and teenage Stephen Sondheim, the genesis of E.B. White's book 'Stuart Little', and Bobby Cannavale's play as the bartender. It's like a sad, single-set 'My dinner with Andre', with an uncanny 5 foot short Ethan Hawke disappointed with his life - in a bar.

I never saw the play 'Oklahoma', but like Hart, I hated the movie version of it.

🍿

"*Tommy! I commend you to open this door..."*

**THE MASTERMIND** (2025), my 5th by minimalist Indie director Kelly Reichardt. A low-key, understated "heist" story, taking place in small-time 1970's New England. Josh O'Connor plays another unusual thief (After his role in 'La Chimera')'. Here he is a clueless, lazy, hapless slacker, who cooks up a cockamamie scheme to steal some random paintings from the local art museum. But he screws up everything in his life, including with his wife, kids and family, by 'not thinking things through'.

Alana Haim, who plays his wife, doesn't have much to do here, but she gives such a "different" vibe as an actress. [*Female Director*]

🍿

"*We're a culture of enormous violence...*"

**COVER UP** (2025) is the latest documentary from Laura Poitras (Director of 'Citizenfour'). A fascinating portrait of investigative journalist Seymour Hersh, who reported on the Mai Lai massacre, Vietnam, Watergate scandal, The CIA CHAOS program, Gulf and Western, Abu Ghraib torture prison, the ethnic cleansing in Gaza, and many other fraud, abuse and corruption cases. It's the American Murder Inc. machine, chemical weaponry, genocide, regime change, extreme prejudices, cruelty and death, all in the name of democracy, the capitalist way of life, and 'fighting Communism'. People have a hard time accepting even with all the evidence that the USA had been the Bad Guys for a long time.

💯 score on Rotten Tomatoes. [*Female Director*]

🍿

"*Adolf Hitler is dead*"

The new "historical drama" **NUREMBERG** was so offensively bad, that I decided to bail on it 3-4 times during the 2.5 long hours of its low-brow, stupid length. But masochistically, I finished it. I thought of watching Ari Folman's acclaimed 'Saint Clara', or even re-watching his 'Waltz with Bashir', but unfortunately, I stumbled on his terrible **THE CONGRESS** (2013) instead.

It's a frustrating live-action/animation hybrid, an adaptation of Stanisław Lem's sci-fi novel. Robyn Wright is playing a version of herself as an aging movie star who lets the studio scan and clone her, so that she can spend her days with her sick son. It's an okay concept, and it was used brilliantly in 'Being John Malkovich'. Here it employs wooden, unconvincing acting, dime store philosophy, and cliched trops. Even Harvey Keitel is terrible. The second half takes place in another dimension, a weird animated universe, a potpourri of psychedelic styles, from Dave Fleischer to Yellow Submarine, sprinkled with pop culture Easter eggs, from Dr. Strangelove to psychotropic drugs to Dylan and Grace Jones. But this confused criticism of the Entertainment-A.I.-Complex was simply not very good. 3/10.

🍿

**THE SHORTS:**

🍿 [**THINKING MOLECULES OF TITAN**](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=DGpJlPBsT0w) (2014) is an ambitious 18-min sci-fi short based on an unfinished [short story by Roger Ebert.](https://www.rogerebert.com/chazs-blog/the-thinking-molecules-of-titan-rogers-unfinished-sci-fi-tale) He wrote it just before his death, and the film is dedicated to his memory. For somebody like me who doesn't appreciate science-fiction, it's flawed, but it must be a "Must" for any Ebert fan. For some reason, it's completely unknown, and has only 1 review on Letterboxd.

🍿 **SOLAR WALK** (2018) is the last trippy film I haven't seen yet by my favorite Hungarian animator, Réka Bucsi (The creator of [Symphony No. 42](https://vimeo.com/153887315)!) It's like a feminine, unpretentious, low-tech "Star Gate" from '2001, A Space Odyssey'. Abstract, geometrical, utterly inventive. [*Female Director*]

🍿 **STORM IN POLAND** is an award-winning, wordless (but with thundering music score) documentary about some serious floods that affected big parts of the Polish countryside in 1947.

🍿 **CAR PARK** (2025), a chubby guy meets a mysterious stranger at a clandestine rendezvous at night, for some possibly nefarious reasons.

🍿 **THE GIFT OF GIVING** (2022), a German short about an old man who works as mall Santa Claus.

3. **Rags (1915, dir. James Kirkwood Sr.)**

Great character study about the perseverance of goodness in a less-than-good environment. The plot is kind of meandering and full of "and then" storytelling, but Mary Pickford's titular character is surprisingly complex and affecting for silent era film. I was really rooting for her the whole time.

**Misery (1990, dir. Rob Reiner)**

When Reiner passed earlier this month, I knew we'd lost a true talent. He was one of the great genre-savvy directors of his time, capable of making great comedies, adventure movies, and coming-of-age stories without skipping a beat. Misery has been on my watchlist for a while now, but upon this retrospective I thought now was the best time to see if his skill could translate to something a lot more harrowing than the Cliffs of Insanity or the drummer's seat at a Spinal Tap concert. And it could. I knew more than a bit about Misery's broad plot movements well before seeing the film, but it was still a masterclass in tension and writing. Kathy Bates and James Caan's performances were simply excellent, and although the clever dialogue ensured that the film moved at a reasonable, entertaining pace, it never lost that crucial sense of claustrophobic, overwhelming dread.

**McCabe and Mrs. Miller (1971, dir. Robert Altman)**

An amazing picture. Through his titular characters, Altman tells nothing less than the story of America itself, where foolhardy individualism and communal thinking meet, clash, and mingle on the muddy, unforgiving, ponderously sublime frontier. The characters' complexities are apparent but never overbearing, and gorgeous cinematography, careful pacing, a director's obeisance to the whims of nature, and no small amount of wit create a compelling world for them to run around in. I only watched this movie last Tuesday, but I have a feeling that in six months I'll be clamoring to rewatch it.

**Alien 3 (1992, dir. David Fincher)**

I really love Alien and Aliens, but have always kept my distance from Alien 3 because of its inciting incident. When I finally sat down to watch it, I was expecting a miserable slog, and while Alien 3 lacks even a semblance of optimism, the film was a lot better than I was expecting - although it's still not very good. Alien 3 tries to subvert the formula established by Aliens, such as when the xeno kills a Hicks-analogue and a Burke-analogue in the space of five minutes, which is a theoretically smart idea, as the audience knows better than to warm up to a nice character if he can just die offscreen before the movie really starts. However, the rest of the film more or less plays out the way you'd expect an Alien sequel to play out, only Ripley is a lot less interesting and you the audience member aren't too invested in whether or not she gets off the planet. The film sort of flounders as an Alien movie, but as an independent sci-fi movie about religious prisoners stuck in a lead refinery, it has some interesting things going on, and Alien 3 as a whole is incredibly well-made. I was shocked when I saw that it was Fincher's debut directorial credit. He really knocked it out of the park.

**Regeneration (1915, dir. Raoul Walsh)**

Similar to Rags, Regeneration tells the story about a young person who must challenge his negative influences to lead a productive life. Whereas Mary Pickford played an ostensibly good person getting shat on by society, Rockliffe Fellowes plays Owen, an ostensibly bad person who redeems himself over the course of the film, abandoning his role in New York's gangster hierarchy in favor of a lawful life with his girlfriend. Owen is a pretty good character, portrayed well by three actors of varying ages, but the story in which he inhabits and the characters he interacts with are by and large less interesting, hampered by weak motivations. The film ran the risk of becoming a real slog if Walsh's directing weren't top notch. There are some great shots, and plenty of big sequences, like street brawls and ferry fires, that are frenetic but never confusing. Even if the narrative loses you every once in a while, Walsh is there to vivify your attention.
4. More re-watches this week. This time, mostly movies I’ve already posted reviews of before, but with two new additions.

**Eyes Wide Shut (1999, Stanley Kubrick) — re-watch** — I think *Eyes Wide Shut* might be the only Kubrick movie so far that’s gone down in my estimation on re-watch. I thought its slow-burn style worked well at building intrigue the first time I watched it, but now that I already know how little the movie ever actually explains about anything, most of the build-up just feels like a tedious waste of time. There are enough promising moments here that one can easily imagine Kubrick might have made a great movie out of it if he had lived, but I can only review the movie that actually exists. **7/10**

**Scrooge (1951, Brian Desmond Hurst) — re-watch** — I think I was a little generous giving this a 9/10 the first time I reviewed it. I still think it’s probably the most dramatically effective version of the story I’ve yet seen — including the original novella — and yet still, outside of Sim’s great performance, it still feels like there’s not that much to it. Dropping this down to an **8/10**.

**Spider-Man 2 (2004, Sam Raimi) — re-watch** — In hindsight, I really don’t know why I was so positive about this movie the first time I reviewed it. I guess I was grading it on a curve compared to other superhero movies, a genre I’m no great fan of in general; but, honestly, even by that standard, it still doesn’t seem *that* good. Raimi knows how to direct the hell out of an action sequence, but in-between the set pieces, there’s just way too much time spent on character drama scenes that are almost uniformly terrible. None of the drama around Peter trying to maintain his friendships with MJ and Harry feels like it has any weight, because it doesn’t actually seem like any of them like each other to begin with. The whole movie, every time we see either of them with Peter, all they do is flip their shit at him over everything he says and does. Why does he want to remain friends with them so badly anyway? Downgrading this to a **6/10**.

**How Green Was My Valley (1941, John Ford) — re-watch** — Still a very impressive work, though perhaps not quite worthy of the 10/10 I gave it in my initial review. Certainly, it is an immaculately made film, even more so than usual for John Ford; if one puts aside the urge to compare it unfavourably to *Citizen Kane*, it’s not hard to see why it was such a massive hit with the awards bodies of its day. At the same time, watching it now, it’s also a lot easier to admire than it is to love; the audience for a movie about the death of the mining economy in 19th-century Wales is only ever going to be so large. I could fairly easily justify calling this a 9/10 must-watch on the strength of its craftsmanship, but if I’m honest about how I actually feel about it, I think I would “only” give it a highly recommended **8/10**.

**War of the Worlds (2005, Steven Spielberg) — re-watch** — The first time I reviewed this movie, I defended it against those who compared it to a Michael Bay- or Roland Emmerich-style “dumb blockbuster”, arguing that it was recognizably the work of the same director who made *Schindler’s List* and *Saving Private Ryan*. While I still stand by thinking that the former comparisons aren’t giving the movie enough credit, I think I overcorrected a little by making the latter claim; it would probably be more accurate to say that this is Spielberg’s version of a latter-career James Cameron movie. Like *Titanic* and *Avatar*, *War of the Worlds* is clearly the work of a top-tier filmmaking talent, but it’s talent that’s applied as a blunt instrument more than an artist’s tool. There’s no subtlety or nuance to any of the characters or themes, it’s all about pummelling the viewer into submission by expressing all its ideas in the biggest, loudest way possible. It does work, at least well enough that I came away loving it on the initial viewing, and I don’t exactly regret my earlier statements; but it does mean that, as Spielberg’s movies go, this is not one of the more satisfying ones to revisit. **7/10**

**Clueless (1995, Amy Heckerling) — re-watch** — Pretty close to being as good as I remembered. Looking back on it, the 9/10 rating I gave it the first time I watched it feels a *little* high for something so fluffy and insubstantial, but the fact remains that this movie is still really goddamn hilarious. Highly recommended. **8/10**

**Avatar (2009, James Cameron) — re-watch** — Speaking of James Cameron, while I do think *Avatar* is a better movie than most people seem to give it credit for nowadays, visiting it this week did help me to understand the detractors are coming from a little better.

In particular, I feel like I can now understand why it became so common to compare this movie unfavourably to *Titanic*, something I’d previously found completely incomprehensible. I could understand liking them both for their stunning technical achievements, and I could also understand disliking them both for their admittedly thin and cliché-filled scripts; and I could also understand disliking *Titanic* — as I do — while preferring *Avatar* because of its better pacing, and for at least attempting to do something interesting with its cliches by moving them to an unfamiliar setting. But until this re-watch, I didn’t really get why someone would dislike *Avatar* while preferring *Titanic*. Now, I think it mostly just comes down to Jake and Neytiri being a weak central couple. None of the characters in *Titanic* were particularly interesting or well-written characters to begin with, but Rose and Jack at least had enough identifiable traits that you could tell what kind of fantasy they were supposed to be pandering to. In *Avatar*, the *villains* are sketched out as broad archetypes — “greedy evil corporate guy” and “hyper-macho evil military guy” — but the heroes don’t even seem to get that. For most of the movie, Jake’s character seems to consist of “guy to whom exposition is delivered”.

Despite this substantial problem, I still love the movie, though. In an era of filmmaking where blockbusters so often feel like they’re all just the same thing over and over, I can’t bring myself to dislike one of the few big-budget films that fully commits to showing you a world unlike anything you’ve seen before, and does it so gorgeously. Highly recommended. **8/10**

**Movie of the week:** *How Green Was My Valley*

## 5. World Cinema

**Score:** 0
**Body:** When did the category come into being? With the Internet or the Netflixication of film? Without it I'd be as ignorant as a door mouse and as shallow as a puddle in the desert. 

I watched The Legend of the Vagabond Queen of Lagos recently and it dawned on me how much I experience of the world through cinema, much more than through literature. 

My preference for decades has been foreign films. World cinema takes it to an entirely new level. It was in the early nineties when I experienced my first Asian cinema. I don't remember if it was a Chinese or Japanese film festival but I recall being utterly captivated by this new way of seeing and seeing the world cinematically. I wasn't living in the US at the time, and I doubt the large city I had been living in would have run films like these. Only a few theaters ran foreign films at all, and those were rather mainstream like Three Colors Blue, which brings me full circle to the notion of experiencing cinema from around the world. 

Clearly, the internet makes that possible, but as I've experienced it the last 30 years, when you're in the US, the world beyond its borders is, I'd say filtered. I'm assuming it's all about the market, but it's also about the cultural politics of the US. And the latter is significant to me because cinema is such a powerful medium. 

When using a VPN with Mubi for example, I find the selection in say Romania more "of the world" than that in the US. But then again, Vagabond Queen of Lagos I watched on Kanopy. I wonder what I'd find using a streaming service not of US origins. And perhaps in the days of theaters, cultural hubs like New York teamed with cinema from around the world unlike any other in the US or elsewhere. Or perhaps the category "World Cinema" is just another way of funnel cultural artifacts into commercial buckets for consumption.

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. US centric answer:

I’m not sure I totally understand what you’re trying to say. “World cinema” has existed since the beginning of cinema. You can find plenty of interviews where filmmakers from the 1960s and 1970s reference being inspired by foreign films from 10-20 years ago. Martin Scorsese being touched at a young age seeing Satyajit Ray films at the cinema, well outside of his Italian background. To whatever extent “world cinema” and “foreign films” aren’t the same thing (which is where I’m getting confused), Scorsese has always been passionate about making films from other countries feel less Other and promoting inclusivity/shared experiences. 

Living in Manhattan you’re surrounded by all sorts of cinema, and living in most other places that’s not the case. I would definitely give credit to Criterion before Netflix as sort of the modern innovator of bringing global cinema to normal people. Before streaming existed, the best way watch a film from another country in the heartland US was buying a criterion DVD from Barnes and Noble. They’re euro-biased, but not euro-centric. Even in the olden times when the collection was only a couple hundred movies, plenty of those were from Kurosawa or Ozu, they had a couple selections from Hong Kong and India and the USSR.

I think the Romania point is an odd one, because Romania just isn’t a comparable country to the US. There’s enough movies and television being made in the US to satiate the average American. Most of the audience will never watch a thing with subtitles and will be absolutely fine with that. Romania (talking out of my ass, not from experience) doesn’t have nearly that much production capability. Even in a best case scenario, Radu Jude makes 2 movies a year and they’re the most popular thing on Romanian Netflix, you’d still need to import from other nearby countries to have a decent streaming service. It makes perfect sense to me that Romanian Netflix has a much higher % of content that’s not Romanian than Netflix US has of non-American content, but I don’t think that means anything except one country is very big and the other is very small.
2. You may find this extremely difficult to believe, but many, many things existed before the Internet...

Including international arts and culture. 

Compulsive logging and competitive consumption were also regrettably present but nowhere near the epidemic levels they have sadly reached today. Perhaps we can hold the Internet responsible for that sickness...
3. From an European world view, it used to be anything not produced in Western-Europe (before the Wall fell) or in the USA.  I remember going to World Cinema Festivals in the early eighties and there were a lot of Eastern European pictures show, more than anything else.  The few others would have been "Arabian" or African movies.

Since then it has changed to mainly mean Asian in it broadest term meaning from the Middle East to Japan, and African cinema.  Everything in Europe is now not considered World Cinema,

5. Probably after the mass adoption of "World Music" to refer to 3rd world/indigenous styles of percussion and melody. I believe this was pioneered by David Byrne and Brian Eno for the marketing of their album "my life in the bush of ghosts," generally commodifying many genres into this label to sell them to Western audiences. In turn, this created interested markets to consume third world media in an effort to be "worldly" and well rounded assumedly also extending to foreign film.
